target.name: Fire Point
target.founder: Denys Shtilerman, Iryna Terekh, Yehor Skalyha

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 8 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Defense & Security. Range: $125M - $3.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

private.name: NODA AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series A), post-money $125M

data.narrative:
Fire Point, a Series A Defense & Security company founded by Denys Shtilerman, Iryna Terekh, and Yehor Skalyha, is evaluated against a comparable universe of 8 private peers and 2 public companies in the same sector and stage cohort. The private peer set includes firms at Series B stage such as Dream Security ($3.0B), Harmattan AI ($1.4B), Code Metal ($1.2B), Castelion ($1.0B), and Defense Unicorns ($1.0B), alongside emerging players like NODA AI, Anduril Industries, Helsing, Shield AI, and Saronic.

Peer-median valuation analysis, the primary triangulation method applied given data availability, yields a median implied valuation of $1.0B across the 8 comparable firms. The peer distribution spans $125M to $3.0B, reflecting heterogeneity in capital deployment and market positioning within the frontier defense-tech space. This median aligns with the concentration of Series B valuations in the $1.0B–$1.4B range, suggesting market-clearing expectations for capital-efficient, early-stage operators in this sector.

The triangulated valuation range of $850M to $1.1B reflects defensible positioning near peer median, with the $1.0B midpoint as the central estimate. Key uncertainties include limited methodological breadth—reliance on single peer-median approach—and Fire Point's nascent revenue profile relative to later-stage comparables. Stage-discount mechanics relative to Series B peers warrant further clarity on growth trajectory and customer concentration, particularly given the undisclosed capital raise and Ukrainian MoD anchor relationship.
